teams.name: Mexico
teams.style: Pragmatic 4-3-3 with a compact mid-block, direct pacey wingers and strong set pieces
keyPlayers: Raúl Jiménez (Fulham, ST — scored in the opener)
teams.form: Unbeaten through 2026 and reigning Gold Cup/Nations League champions; opened the World Cup with a 2-0 win over South Africa at the Azteca.
teams.outlook: Altitude, home noise and Jiménez's form make them group favorites (Opta 48%), but creativity vs low blocks is thin and César Montes is suspended for the South Korea game.

teams.name: South Korea
teams.style: Flexible 4-2-3-1/3-4-3 built around Kim Min-jae, organized mid-block with rapid vertical transitions
keyPlayers: Son Heung-min (LAFC, FW, captain — 9 goals in 15 MLS games in 2026)
Lee Kang-in (PSG, AM — assisted the equalizer vs Czechia)
Hwang In-beom (Feyenoord, CM — goal and assist vs Czechia)
teams.form: Only unbeaten AFC qualifier (6W 4D); came from behind to beat the Czech Republic 2-1 on matchday one.
teams.outlook: Elite spine and proven resilience, but heavy reliance on Kim Min-jae's fitness and Son's 33-year-old legs, with finishing efficiency a nagging worry.

teams.name: Czech Republic
teams.style: Pragmatic, physical low-block 3-4-2-1; the best set-piece team in UEFA qualifying, weaponizing Coufal's long throws
keyPlayers: Patrik Schick (Bayer Leverkusen, ST — 9 goals in his last 8 club games)
Ladislav Krejčí (Wolves, CB — scored vs Korea)
teams.form: Reached a first World Cup since 2006 via playoff shootout wins over Ireland and Denmark; won both June friendlies before the 2-1 loss to South Korea.
teams.outlook: Schick's red-hot form and dead-ball menace give a real third-place-or-better path, but open-play creativity is thin and the back line lacks pace.

teams.name: South Africa
teams.style: Disciplined 4-3-3 low-mid block with quick counters, built on a cohesive Mamelodi Sundowns core
keyPlayers: Teboho Mokoena (Mamelodi Sundowns, CM, set-piece specialist)
teams.form: Won CAF Group C, but exited AFCON in the last 16, drew Nicaragua 0-0, then lost 2-0 to Mexico with two red cards.
teams.outlook: Organized, cohesive and well-goalkept, but the attack is blunt and the suspensions of Sithole and Zwane gut them for the must-win Czechia game.

groups.groupReading: The Azteca's mountain oracle spoke twice and Mexico heard it both times — six points from two wins makes El Tri the tournament's first qualifiers, their thin-air home advantage transforming every page of the group. The crystal's second chapter fulfilled: South Korea's swift spirits hold three points and need only a draw in Monterrey to advance. The Czechs and Bafana share a single point apiece after Schick's flame found its wall in Atlanta — the June 24 finale is the last gate for either to slip through as a best-third pilgrim. The order likely holds; only a dramatic southern night rewrites it.

matches.reading: The orb glowed over the Azteca before a ball was kicked... thin air at 2,240 metres and eighty thousand voices were always going to smother a Bafana side that creates little against elite blocks. Quiñones' strike and Jiménez's header shimmer in the glass — though three red mists swirled that no seer could love.
matches.keyFactor: Altitude and Azteca crowd overwhelm South Africa

matches.reading: The mist parts to show Coufal's long throw thundering in and Krejčí rising — yet the spirits favor the silkier feet. Hwang In-beom's Feyenoord form burns brightest; Korea's transitions wear down a Czech back line short of pace before the night grows old, even as Son's chances flicker and fade.
matches.keyFactor: Korea's transition pace against a slow Czech back three

matches.reading: Schick's flame — nine goals in his last eight for Leverkusen — licks at a South African block stripped of the suspended Sithole and Zwane. The bones grant Mokoena one thunderbolt from a dead ball, but the tall ones from Prague score twice in Atlanta's sealed noon heat and end Bafana's road.
matches.keyFactor: South Africa's suspensions meet Schick's hot streak

matches.reading: Two winners circle each other in Guadalajara's night and the crystal clouds, for Montes' suspension leaves a door ajar for Son's diagonal runs. Yet Jiménez's gravity, Lozano's directness and a second home cauldron tilt the glass — a narrow El Tri verdict, though a draw whispers loudly from the edges.
matches.keyFactor: Home cauldron vs Mexico's makeshift, Montes-less defense

matches.reading: Mexico arrive as Group A's first qualifiers — Aguirre will rotate, handing minutes to Giménez and Charly Rodríguez while Jiménez and Lozano observe from the bench. The Azteca never sleeps, however, and Schick — against a rotated backline at altitude — is the one variable the glass treats with genuine respect; his nine-goal Leverkusen run does not care which central defender faces him. One Czech thunderbolt from a set piece flickers in the mist; Mexico's depth, even rotated, answers twice as the thin Azteca air keeps favouring the crimson shirt.
matches.keyFactor: Mexico rotating with qualification sealed vs Schick's set-piece threat and Czech desperation for a third-place spot

matches.reading: In Monterrey's furnace South Korea need only a draw to advance — three points to South Africa's one, the arithmetic governing every nerve of Hong Myung-bo's setup. Broos must win AND hope Czech Republic's Schick punishes Mexico's rotation: a slim path, but the only gate Bafana have left. Son's movement along the stretched back line and Kim Min-jae's aerial authority against Foster and Mofokeng's pace tilt the glass; Mokoena's long-range thunder delivers the one South African moment the mist grants, but Korea seal the evening with enough composure that the qualifying question is never genuinely in doubt.
matches.keyFactor: South Korea needing only a draw vs South Africa needing a win AND Czech help — the arithmetic governs both squads' risk appetite

teams.name: Canada
teams.style: High-octane 4-4-2 'Maplepressing' — aggressive high press, forced turnovers and fast vertical transitions
keyPlayers: Alphonso Davies (Bayern Munich, LB, captain — back from 2025 ACL tear)
teams.form: Seven-game unbeaten run in warm-ups; opened the World Cup with a 1-1 draw vs Bosnia (Lukic corner-kick header threatened, Larin super-sub cameo); then recorded Canada's first-ever men's World Cup win with a historic 6-0 demolition of Qatar — David hat-trick, Larin and Saliba also scored; Ismaël Koné suffered a suspected tibia/fibula fracture in the 51st minute after a hard tackle from Madibo (red card) and is likely out for the tournament.
teams.outlook: Canada have won their first-ever men's World Cup match — the 6-0 rout of Qatar will be recited in Vancouver for generations. Tied on 4 points with Switzerland heading into the June 24 summit at BC Place; Jonathan David's hat-trick and the collective press confirmed everything Marsch has promised. The shadow over the celebration: Koné's fractured leg likely ends his tournament, but the roster depth — Eustáquio as the engine, Larin off the bench — can absorb it. Canada believe; the only question now is first or second in Group B.

teams.name: Switzerland
teams.style: Flexible, possession-oriented 4-2-3-1 flowing through Xhaka, with a miserly mid-block and fast breaks via Ndoye
keyPlayers: Granit Xhaka (Sunderland, CM, captain — record 145 caps, fourth World Cup)
teams.form: Won UEFA qualifying group unbeaten (only 2 conceded); beat Jordan 4-1 and drew Australia 1-1 in warm-ups; opened the World Cup with a 1-1 draw vs Qatar — Embolo penalty 17', Khoukhi equalizer 90+4; then delivered the tournament's most emphatic second-half performance — 4-1 over Bosnia with all four Nati goals scored by substitutes in the final 20 minutes, including a Johan Manzambi brace and Xhaka's penalty.
teams.outlook: The Qatar concentration doubts were emphatically answered against Bosnia: four substitute goals in twenty minutes revealed a bench quality few nations can match at this level. Tied on 4 points with Canada heading into June 24; Xhaka's 145-cap leadership and Kobel's gloves anchor a side commanding their group. Three straight second-place stalls at World Cups — this Swiss squad has the tools to end the pattern.

teams.name: Qatar
teams.style: Spanish-school possession 4-3-3 with Afif free between the lines — open, entertaining and defensively leaky
keyPlayers: Akram Afif (Al Sadd, AM — 11 assists in qualifying)
Almoez Ali (Al Duhail, ST — AFC qualifying top scorer)
teams.form: Winless in six pre-tournament; opened the World Cup with a HISTORIC 1-1 draw vs Switzerland — Khoukhi's 90+4 equalizer earned Qatar their first-ever World Cup point in their 10th attempt; then were swept aside 0-6 by Canada at BC Place — Jonathan David's hat-trick and Mohamed Manai's own goal headlined the largest defeat in Qatar's World Cup history.
teams.outlook: Qatar depart Group B with their one historic point against Switzerland as the chapter's only lasting verse. The 0-6 Canadian rout closed the story entirely; their June 24 finale against Bosnia is a pride match only. Lopetegui's experiment ends here.

teams.name: Bosnia and Herzegovina
teams.style: Compact 4-4-2, direct service into the Džeko–Demirović front two, with a heavy set-piece emphasis
keyPlayers: Edin Džeko (Schalke 04, ST, captain — 40 years old, record 72 international goals)
teams.form: Qualified via March playoff penalty wins over Wales and Italy; drew final warm-up 1-1 with Panama; opened the World Cup with a 1-1 draw vs Canada — Lukic's corner-kick header the standout moment, Džeko's shoulder kept him off the bench; then were swept aside 1-4 by Switzerland — Džeko did return to start but all four Nati goals came from substitutes after the hour as Bosnia's structure collapsed.
teams.outlook: Eliminated from the knockout places. Switzerland's substitute barrage in the final twenty minutes exposed Bosnia's defensive depth limits once their shape broke; Džeko's shoulder allowed him to play but could not change the tide. Their June 24 finale against Qatar is a pride match — a win keeps best-third arithmetic slim but alive; Lukic's set-piece menace remains the one weapon either side carries on that day.

groups.groupReading: The glass was remade twice on June 18: Switzerland's four substitute goals in the final twenty minutes swept Bosnia aside 4-1, and Canada's 6-0 destruction of Qatar wrote history in Vancouver — Jonathan David's hat-trick, Larin's return and Ismaël Koné's fractured leg all woven into Canada's first-ever men's World Cup win in the same extraordinary evening. Both sides now hold four points; Bosnia and Qatar share one apiece and are eliminated. The June 24 summit at BC Place decides only the order, not the passage — Xhaka's final group-stage chapter against Marsch's now-believing Canada. The red and white, once the tournament's longest-suffering, have found their voice.

matches.reading: The maple mist rises over BMO Field even without Davies, whose hamstring keeps the captain robed at kickoff. Marsch's press will harry a Bosnia built around a 40-year-old Džeko nursing a wounded shoulder — yet the old wolf bites once against the run of the cards before David, hungry after a lean Juventus year, settles a nervous home debut.
matches.keyFactor: Canada's press and home crowd vs Džeko's aging but cunning threat

matches.reading: The glass barely fogs here: a Swiss wall that conceded twice in all of qualifying meets a Qatar side winless in six and leaking goals. Ndoye's runs and Embolo's shoulders breach the maroon line either side of the hour; Afif conjures sparks in the Bay Area sun, but no fire.
matches.keyFactor: Switzerland's elite defense against winless, leaky Qatar

matches.reading: Under SoFi's silver roof the runes counsel caution — Xhaka must turn the wheel while Akanji's back line answers questions it never faced in qualifying. Switzerland allowed Khoukhi to equalise in the 94th minute against Qatar, a lapse of concentration that Bosnia's set-piece sorcery — Lukic's corner-kick header against Canada already confirmed the danger — will punish if repeated. Džeko, still nursing the shoulder that kept him on the bench in Toronto, remains the great unknown; if he starts, the aerial menace doubles. Yet Swiss technical superiority and Kobel's gloves are sterner than what a last-gasp Qatari defensive midfielder provided — two Nati strikes on either side of the break, with Bosnia landing one from a dead ball to keep the ledger honest.
matches.keyFactor: Switzerland's concentration after the Qatar lapse vs Bosnia's proven set-piece danger — Džeko shoulder fitness the decisive swing

matches.reading: Vancouver's harbour wind carries red waves — but the orb now reads a Qatar transformed by Khoukhi's last-gasp point against Switzerland. That 90+4 equalizer is not mere fortune; it is proof that Lopetegui's organisation can survive ninety minutes of pressure and bite at the death. Canada, still searching for their first group-stage win after the BMO draw, welcome Davies's return to tip the balance from the left — yet Afif and Almoez Ali will not merely ship goals politely in BC Place's smaller confines. Two Canadian strikes are the glass's verdict, but Qatar's belief earns one back before the final whistle.
matches.keyFactor: Canada's press and Davies's return vs Qatar's newly-found World Cup resolve — no longer a foregone conclusion

matches.reading: The scales still tremble, but both keepers of this gate hold four points and advance regardless of the scoreline; the spirits smell cautious joy and the shadow of Koné's empty midfield seat. Kobel's gloves against Davies' lightning, Xhaka's 145-cap tempo against Marsch's now-historic storm — Canada without their injured engine may court the same careful draw that suits both sides. The orb still shows a single strike apiece and handshakes at the whistle, with first place likely decided on goal difference: Canada's +5 cushion over Switzerland's +3 tilts the banner maple-red.
matches.keyFactor: Both sides already through on 4 points; a draw suits both — Koné's absence the one change that could tip the tempo to Switzerland

matches.reading: Seattle's grey skies host a final act between two eliminated sides: Džeko proved fit enough to start against Switzerland but Bosnia's structure still conceded four; Qatar's 0-6 humiliation in Vancouver left nothing of Lopetegui's organisational promise. The shootout-forged nerve survives in the Bosnian dressing room — Lukic's set-piece menace and Džeko's pride carry them to a win over a demoralised Maroon squad, but no six-goal swing arrives to save either side's third-place arithmetic. One moment of quality settles it and the Group B story closes with Bosnia's heads higher than Qatar's.
matches.keyFactor: Bosnia's Lukic set-piece danger and Džeko's pride vs a Qatar side stripped of confidence after the 0-6 Vancouver rout

teams.name: Brazil
teams.style: Fluid 4-2-3-1/4-3-3 with an all-out front line — Vinícius left, Raphinha right — and rotating midfield shapes
keyPlayers: Vinícius Júnior (Real Madrid, FW — 22 goals in 53 club games in 2025-26)
Raphinha (Barcelona, W — 21 goals and 7 assists in 33 matches)
Matheus Cunha (Wolverhampton, ST — brace vs Haiti filling the Neymar void)
teams.form: Won four of last six pre-tournament; drew 1-1 with Morocco in the opener at MetLife — conceded Saibari's strike in the 21st minute before Vinícius equalized in the 32nd; then throttled Haiti 3-0 in Philadelphia — Cunha's brace and Vinícius's strike with Neymar (calf) still sidelined.
teams.outlook: Group C leaders on goal difference over Morocco (GD +3 vs +1), both on 4 points. Brazil need only a draw against Scotland in Miami on June 24 to advance — Ancelotti can park pragmatically in front of the back line, making Scotland's must-win task even harder. Cunha's emergence deepens the attack, though the back line's susceptibility to pace remains the known flaw.

teams.name: Morocco
teams.style: 4-2-3-1 morphing into 4-2-2-2 to open a motorway lane for Hakimi — more expansive and possession-dominant than the Regragui era
keyPlayers: Achraf Hakimi (PSG, RB, captain — reigning African Player of the Year)
Brahim Díaz (Real Madrid, AM — AFCON 2025 Golden Boot)
Ismael Saibari (two World Cup goals in 21 and 70 seconds — the group's most lethal sub-pattern)
teams.form: Perfect 8-for-8 in CAF qualifying, AFCON 2025 champions; drew 1-1 with Brazil in the opener — Saibari opened the scoring in the 21st minute; then beat Scotland 1-0 in Foxborough — Saibari's header landed after just 70 seconds, the fastest World Cup goal since Alphonso Davies vs Croatia in 2022.
teams.outlook: Four points, 2nd in Group C on goal difference to Brazil; Morocco need only avoid defeat vs Haiti on June 24 to advance — even a draw suffices while Scotland cannot overtake them without a Brazil win in the parallel game. Saibari's two goals prove the scorer depth runs deeper than Brahim Díaz alone.

teams.name: Scotland
teams.style: Organized 4-2-3-1 with set-piece strength and big-game midfield runners; can drop to a back five against elite sides
keyPlayers: Scott McTominay (Napoli, CM — 2024-25 Serie A MVP, scored the bicycle kick that sealed qualification)
John McGinn (Aston Villa, CM — scored the winner vs Haiti)
teams.form: First World Cup since 1998; opened the World Cup with a WIN — McGinn's deflected strike beat Haiti 1-0, putting Scotland top of Group C for the first time since 1974; then lost 0-1 to Morocco in Foxborough — Saibari's header landed after 70 seconds (a denied McTominay penalty adding to the frustration); Scotland sit 3rd on 3 points.
teams.outlook: Three points and still alive — but Scotland must WIN against Brazil in Miami on June 24 to advance; a draw is not enough given Morocco's 4-pt lead. Clarke's back five held Haiti comfortably and Morocco only scored from the game's opening set piece, but Vinícius, Cunha and Raphinha represent an exponentially greater attacking threat. McTominay and one moment of set-piece magic remain the orb's only tartan thread.

teams.name: Haiti
teams.style: Compact, counter-attacking underdogs — young diaspora squad (average age 24) with genuine pace in transition
keyPlayers: Duckens Nazon (ST — all-time top scorer with 44 goals)
teams.form: Won their CONCACAF qualifying group despite playing every 'home' game abroad; beat New Zealand 4-0 then lost 2-1 to Peru; lost opener 0-1 to Scotland (McGinn 28') — Pierrot's late header flashed wide; then fell 0-3 to Brazil in Philadelphia — Haiti's pilgrimage ends without a point.
teams.outlook: Eliminated on 0 points after two losses — the pilgrimage ends with dignity and song. The June 24 finale against Morocco is a farewell.

groups.groupReading: The crystal rewrote Group C's second chapter exactly as foretold — but the arithmetic still breathes Scottish fire. Brazil throttled Haiti 3-0 in Philadelphia (Cunha's brace and Vinícius's strike, still without Neymar's calf), and Morocco's Saibari struck after just 70 seconds in Foxborough to deny the Tartan Army with the fastest World Cup goal since Alphonso Davies in 2022. The standings now show Brazil 1st on goal difference (GD +3) and Morocco 2nd (GD +1), both on 4 points; Scotland 3rd on 3 points, still alive but needing a FULL WIN against Brazil in Miami on June 24 — a draw is not enough. Morocco face Haiti and need only avoid defeat to advance, whatever Scotland do. Haiti's pilgrimage ends in the Atlanta farewell. The oracle sees the giants sealing their passage; the tartan flame burns one night longer, knowing the mountain ahead.

matches.reading: The orb churns gold and crimson over MetLife — Vinícius and Raphinha arrive in the form of their lives, yet Brazil have conceded in five straight and Militão's absence gapes at the back. Hakimi's motorway lane carves one answer and Bono stands tall, but the canary firepower edges a duel the bones call nearly even.
matches.keyFactor: Brazil's elite wingers vs their own leaky, depleted backline

matches.reading: Fifty-two years of waiting fills Gillette with Haitian song, yet the tartan spirits march colder and deeper into the night. McTominay's late surge and a set-piece thunderclap appear in the glass; Isidor's pace draws gasps from the mist, but no goals.
matches.keyFactor: Scotland's set pieces and superior squad depth

matches.reading: The Tartan Army return to Foxborough as group leaders — a sentence that has never been written before — but the glass cautions against the champagne. Clarke's back five, disciplined against Haiti's direct threat, now faces Hakimi's motorway lane and Brahim Díaz's left foot, a combination several grades above Nazon and Bellegarde. Without Gilmour's calm feet Scotland must surrender the ball for long ages; one moment of Atlas precision cuts through a Scottish organisation that cannot press for ninety minutes at this level. Morocco win narrowly, and the October-like Gillette night tells Scotland they must win Miami to make history.
matches.keyFactor: Morocco's attacking class — Hakimi and Brahim Díaz — vs Scotland's organised back five without Gilmour's ball progression

matches.reading: Philadelphia's cauldron shows no mercy in the glass: the widest talent gulf in Group C, and Ancelotti's men — already stung by Scotland's early lead in this group — smell a goal-difference feast against the tournament's thinnest squad. A draw against Morocco is not the start Brazil wanted; the response here must be overwhelming. Neymar, if the calf spirits permit, writes a cameo verse, and the canary firepower pours through every gap Haiti cannot close. Four canary strikes shimmer in the crystal, though Haiti's pace on the break conjures one long-distance threat that Cavaleiro's gloves smother.
matches.keyFactor: Brazil needing goals to chase Scotland and Morocco; Haiti's thinnest squad in the field can't resist elite pressing for 90 minutes

matches.reading: Miami's golden hour now holds the full weight of Scottish history: Clarke's back five must WIN — not draw, WIN — against a Brazil side armed with Vinícius, Cunha and Raphinha, who throttled Haiti 3-0 without Neymar even needing to leave the bench. Ancelotti needs only a draw and will flood the midfield accordingly, turning this into a siege Brazil can organise without strain. McTominay's Serie A MVP nerve must conjure not one but two moments of defiance; Che Adams must hold and win headers he has no right to win. One Scottish goal under Miami's lights will make the night immortal — but Brazil's draw arithmetic and individual quality make the second Scottish goal the game's most impossible request. The orb sees the canary answer the tartan early enough that Scotland's tired legs cannot chase it twice.
matches.keyFactor: Scotland must WIN — a draw eliminates them; Brazil need only a draw and will defend that line with Ancelotti's full tactical intelligence and Cunha's fresh legs off the back of a 3-0 win

matches.reading: Atlanta's sealed roof traps the Atlas lions' roar — a side with 4 points and Saibari already the group's fastest striker. Morocco need only a draw to advance regardless of the Scotland-Brazil result; the training ground has not learned to coast. Hakimi marauds down his motorway, Brahim finishes twice, and Bono's clean sheet is etched in the glass before the second candle burns down. Haiti, eliminated and playing for farewell dignity, have earned the right to be respected; the Atlas lion's mercy, however, does not come in the form of missed chances.
matches.keyFactor: Morocco needing only a draw to advance (or a win to claim first if Brazil slip) — against an eliminated Haiti side carrying only pride
